case_name: Pediatrics Cool Care v. Ginger Thompson, Individually and as the Representative of the Estate of A.W.(deceased), and Brad Washington

summary.brief: Pediatrics Cool Care v. Ginger Thompson, Individually and as the Representative of the Estate of A.W.(deceased), and Brad Washington. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: It is undisputed that a thirteen-year-old girl (A.W.) showed up with her mother (Mother) at the Pediatrics Cool Care clinic on March 1, 2012, seeking help for 'severe depression,' an inability to 'control her feelings,' and feeling 'stressed out' and 'sad all the time.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The physician assistant failed to perform a psychiatric work-up, failed to utilize a standard questionnaire for assessing depression in adolescents, failed to adequately interview A.W., failed to attempt to talk to her without Mother present, and failed to ask her to promise that she would tell someone if she ever felt like hurting herself.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A.W. returned to the clinic complaining of migraines on April 17, 2012, and was seen only by a nurse practitioner.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Moss then explained that the physician assistant’s failure to appropriately and adequately evaluate A.W.’s condition and its causes prevented the defendants from identifying the treatment options they should have pursued.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Fred Moss

evidence_index.text: The Court agrees, but I do not.
evidence_index.speaker: Justice Boyd dissenting

evidence_index.text: The Court first concludes that the court of appeals erred by requiring only that the defendants’ negligence was a 'substantial factor' in causing A.W.’s suicide when it should have required the evidence to also show that A.W. would not have committed suicide 'but for' the defendants’ negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Court

evidence_index.text: Dr. Moss testified that, based on reasonable medical probability, the defendants’ actions—and more importantly, their failures to take actions they should have taken—created a 'cluster of factors' that more likely than not caused A.W. to commit suicide.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Fred Moss

evidence_index.text: Dr. Moss then concluded his direct examination by repeating his 'firm opinion,' based on reasonable medical probability, that the defendants’ failures to adequately interview, evaluate, and follow up with A.W. more likely than not caused A.W.’s suicide.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Fred Moss

evidence_index.text: The defendants complain—and the Court agrees—that Dr. Moss’s testimony was insufficient because he could not identify exactly what information the defendants would have obtained from A.W. had they properly interviewed, evaluated, and followed up with her.
evidence_index.speaker: Court

evidence_index.text: The Court, however, concludes that the record contains legally insufficient evidence because Dr. Moss’s testimony was conclusory and mere 'speculation' based on numerous 'ifs.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: Family history of depression and bipolar disorder

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe depression, inability to control feelings, feeling stressed out and sad all the time
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient has been depressed for some time, with a family history of depression and bipolar disorder. Mother is taking Celexa for depression.

focused_exam.psychiatric: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

critical_findings: Family history of depression and bipolar disorder

requestables.name: Psychiatric Evaluation
requestables.description: Comprehensive psychiatric work-up including standard questionnaire for depression

reveal.decision_impact: Missed opportunity to assess severity and risk of self-harm

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to clinic with severe depression

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up Visit
timeline_phases.description: Patient returns with migraines, mood reportedly improved

key_events.event: Seen by nurse practitioner, Celexa continued

decision_points.prompt: What should be the initial management plan for a 13-year-old with severe depression?

options.description: Conduct a thorough psychiatric evaluation and refer to a mental health specialist
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for severe depression includes comprehensive evaluation and specialist referral
Inadequate assessment and management for severe depression

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Physician assistant did not perform a psychiatric work-up

expected_action_court.description: Conduct a thorough psychiatric evaluation and refer to a mental health specialist
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for comprehensive evaluation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to perform psychiatric evaluation and refer to specialist
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Comprehensive evaluation and referral to mental health services
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to perform psychiatric evaluation
Causation established - failure to follow up and evaluate contributed to suicide

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to perform psychiatric evaluation and follow-up
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper evaluation and follow-up could have prevented suicide

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to clinic with severe depression
Patient returns with migraines, mood reportedly improved

allegations.allegation: Failure to perform psychiatric evaluation and follow-up

court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to perform psychiatric evaluation and follow-up
